title: Build and Print a Dynamic 2D Array
prompt_md:
## Goal

The dimensions of a grid are only known at runtime. Build a dynamic 2D array of ints using a pointer-to-pointer (`int**`): an array of row pointers, each pointing to its own dynamically allocated row. Fill it from input, print it back, and free every allocation.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `rows` and `cols` (1 <= rows, cols <= 10).
- Next `rows` lines: `cols` space-separated integers each.

## Output

- The grid, one row per line, values separated by a single space (no trailing space), newline after each row.

## Constraints

- Build the grid with `int**` and `new` (one allocation for the row pointers plus one per row). Free the rows, then the row-pointer array. No `std::vector` or other containers.

title: Transpose a Dynamic Matrix
prompt_md:
## Goal

Read an `R x C` matrix into a dynamic 2D array, then compute its **transpose** into a *second* dynamic 2D array and print that. The transpose is a `C x R` matrix in which the value at row `r`, column `c` of the original lands at row `c`, column `r` of the result.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `R` and `C` (1 <= R, C <= 10).
- Next `R` lines: `C` space-separated integers each.

## Output

- The transposed matrix: `C` rows of `R` values, single space between values (no trailing space), newline after each row.

## Constraints

- Both matrices are dynamic `int**` allocations. The transpose must be stored in its own `C x R` array, not printed straight from the original. Free both completely (rows first, then the row-pointer arrays). No `std::vector` or other containers.

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single row becomes single column
